report_metadata.cancer_type: Myoepithelial Carcinoma of the Parotid Gland
report_metadata.stage: T3N1M0 — Locally advanced with nodal involvement
report_metadata.clinical_context: Myoepithelial carcinoma (malignant myoepithelioma) of the parotid gland in a 45-year-old male. T3N1M0 with EWSR1 rearrangement and Ki-67 of 25%, indicating high-grade biology. This is a rare salivary gland malignancy (<2% of all salivary gland tumors) with sarcoma-like gene fusions. Limited evidence base due to extreme rarity. Should be classified as high-grade malignancy. Treatment involves radical parotidectomy with neck dissection followed by adjuvant radiation therapy. Chemotherapy is empiric — doxorubicin/ifosfamide sarcoma-type regimens may be considered given the EWSR1 fusion and sarcoma-like biology. Ki-67 of 25% correlates with worse prognosis and supports aggressive multimodality treatment. 5-year OS approximately 70%. High rates of distant metastasis (22-33%) and local recurrence (30-41%). Molecular profiling should be pursued for potential basket trial enrollment.

treatments.name: Radical Parotidectomy with Ipsilateral Neck Dissection

evidence_level.rationale: ASCO 2021 and ESMO/EURACAN 2022 guidelines recommend surgical resection as primary treatment for resectable salivary gland malignancies. Multiple retrospective series confirm surgery as mainstay of therapy. For T3N1 disease, radical parotidectomy with neck dissection is standard. No RCTs exist due to disease rarity.

survival_benefit.rationale: 5-year OS approximately 70% for myoepithelial carcinoma overall. Complete resection with negative margins provides the best chance of cure. T3N1 with high Ki-67 confers worse prognosis than early-stage disease. pN+ status is an independent predictor of decreased survival.

accessibility.rationale: Standard head and neck surgical procedure available at all major surgical centers. Requires experienced head and neck surgeon for facial nerve management and comprehensive neck dissection. Widely available.

safety_profile.rationale: Facial nerve sacrifice may be necessary for T3 disease with nerve involvement. Risk of permanent facial palsy. Other risks include Frey syndrome, shoulder dysfunction (if spinal accessory nerve affected during neck dissection), wound infection, seroma, hematoma.

biomarker_match.rationale: Universally applicable to all resectable myoepithelial carcinomas regardless of molecular profile. EWSR1 rearrangement status does not alter surgical indication.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Complete surgical excision of the primary tumor in the parotid gland with adequate margins, including sacrifice of the facial nerve if involved by tumor. Radical parotidectomy removes the entire parotid gland with surrounding tissue. Comprehensive neck dissection (levels I-V or selective levels I-III, IV, V) addresses clinically involved lymph nodes (N1). Intraoperative frozen section margin analysis guides extent of resection.

key_evidence.study_name: Risk Factors and Prognosis for Myoepithelial Carcinoma of the Major Salivary Glands

biomarker_requirements: No specific biomarker required for surgical eligibility
notable_side_effects: Facial nerve paralysis (temporary or permanent depending on nerve involvement)
treatments.availability: Widely available at head and neck surgical centers

treatments.name: Adjuvant Radiation Therapy (IMRT) — Post-Surgical

evidence_level.rationale: ASCO and ESMO guidelines recommend adjuvant RT for high-risk salivary gland cancers including T3/T4 tumors, N+ disease, high-grade histology, perineural invasion, and close/positive margins. NCDB analysis demonstrates improved survival with adjuvant RT for high-risk salivary gland malignancies (HR 0.76, p=0.002).

survival_benefit.rationale: NCDB analysis showed adjuvant RT is associated with improved survival in high-risk salivary gland malignancies. For T3N1 high-grade myoepithelial carcinoma, adjuvant RT addresses microscopic residual disease and reduces locoregional recurrence. Local recurrence rate is 30-41% without adjuvant therapy.

accessibility.rationale: IMRT widely available at all radiation oncology centers. Standard treatment planning and delivery. Typical dose: 60-66 Gy in 30-33 fractions.

safety_profile.rationale: Parotid bed RT causes xerostomia (contralateral parotid sparing with IMRT), mucositis, dermatitis, trismus, dental complications. Risk of osteoradionecrosis. Manageable but significant long-term effects.

biomarker_match.rationale: Indicated for all high-risk salivary gland malignancies regardless of molecular profile. T3N1 with Ki-67 25% strongly supports adjuvant RT indication.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: High-energy photon beams delivered via intensity-modulated radiation therapy (IMRT) target the surgical bed, parotid bed, and involved neck levels. Radiation causes DNA double-strand breaks in residual microscopic tumor cells, leading to cell death. IMRT optimizes dose distribution to target while sparing contralateral parotid, brainstem, spinal cord, and other critical structures.

key_evidence.study_name: Adjuvant radiation for salivary gland malignancies is associated with improved survival: NCDB analysis

biomarker_requirements: High-risk features: T3/T4, N+, high-grade, perineural invasion, close/positive margins
All features present in this patient
notable_side_effects: Xerostomia (mitigated by IMRT parotid-sparing technique for contralateral gland)
treatments.availability: Widely available — standard radiation oncology procedure

treatments.name: Doxorubicin + Ifosfamide (Sarcoma-Type Chemotherapy) — Empiric Adjuvant/Salvage

evidence_level.rationale: No RCT data specific to myoepithelial carcinoma. Rationale based on sarcoma-like EWSR1 fusion biology. Case reports demonstrate responsiveness of EWSR1-rearranged myoepithelial carcinoma to sarcoma-type chemotherapy. Doxorubicin/ifosfamide achieves ORR 33-48% in soft tissue sarcomas (ECOG trials).

survival_benefit.rationale: Case report of long-term survival (7.8 years) with doxorubicin/ifosfamide + proton beam for paraspinal myoepithelial carcinoma. ORR 33-48% in soft tissue sarcomas. Given the high distant metastasis rate (22-33%) for myoepithelial carcinoma, systemic therapy may reduce metastatic risk.

accessibility.rationale: Both agents widely available and FDA-approved. Standard sarcoma chemotherapy regimen administered at any oncology center. Well-established dosing protocols.

safety_profile.rationale: Significant toxicity: cardiotoxicity (doxorubicin — cumulative dose limit), hemorrhagic cystitis (ifosfamide — requires mesna), severe myelosuppression, nausea/vomiting, alopecia, neurotoxicity (ifosfamide encephalopathy). Febrile neutropenia risk.

biomarker_match.rationale: EWSR1 rearrangement creates sarcoma-like biology that may predict chemosensitivity to sarcoma regimens. EWSR1-POU5F1 fusion myoepithelial carcinoma has shown responsiveness to VDC/IE regimen. Ki-67 25% suggests sufficient proliferative activity for chemosensitivity.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Doxorubicin intercalates into DNA, inhibits topoisomerase II, and generates free radicals causing DNA damage. Ifosfamide is an alkylating agent that crosslinks DNA strands, preventing DNA replication. The combination provides synergistic cytotoxicity through complementary mechanisms. The sarcoma-type regimen is selected based on the EWSR1 fusion biology shared between myoepithelial carcinoma and soft tissue sarcomas.

key_evidence.study_name: Doxorubicin and ifosfamide with proton beam therapy for myoepithelial carcinoma (case report + literature review)

treatments.availability: Widely available — standard sarcoma chemotherapy regimen

treatments.name: VDC/IE (Vincristine, Doxorubicin, Cyclophosphamide / Ifosfamide, Etoposide) — Ewing Sarcoma Regimen

evidence_level.rationale: Case report of EWSR1-POU5F1 fusion myoepithelial carcinoma highly responsive to VDC/IE regimen. Standard Ewing sarcoma protocol. Theoretical rationale based on shared EWSR1 fusion biology between myoepithelial carcinoma and Ewing sarcoma family tumors.

survival_benefit.rationale: Case report demonstrated high responsiveness of aggressive EWSR1-rearranged myoepithelial carcinoma. VDC/IE achieves high response rates in Ewing sarcoma (60-70%). Unknown generalizability to all EWSR1-rearranged myoepithelial carcinomas.

accessibility.rationale: All agents FDA-approved. Standard protocol used in sarcoma centers. Requires experienced oncology team for administration and toxicity management. Typically administered as alternating cycles.

safety_profile.rationale: Intensive multi-agent chemotherapy with cumulative toxicities: cardiotoxicity (doxorubicin), hemorrhagic cystitis (ifosfamide/cyclophosphamide), severe myelosuppression, neurotoxicity (vincristine), secondary leukemia risk (etoposide). Higher toxicity burden than doxorubicin/ifosfamide doublet.

biomarker_match.rationale: EWSR1 rearrangement creates molecular similarity to Ewing sarcoma family tumors. Case-level evidence of chemosensitivity in EWSR1-POU5F1 fusion myoepithelial carcinoma. Ki-67 25% supports potential chemosensitivity.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Alternating cycles of VDC (vincristine — microtubule disruption, doxorubicin — topoisomerase II inhibition and DNA intercalation, cyclophosphamide — DNA alkylation) and IE (ifosfamide — DNA alkylation, etoposide — topoisomerase II inhibition). This intensive multi-agent regimen targets rapidly dividing cells through multiple complementary mechanisms. Used in Ewing sarcoma and applied empirically to EWSR1-rearranged tumors based on shared molecular biology.

key_evidence.study_name: Aggressive myoepithelial carcinoma with EWSR1-POU5F1 fusion highly responsive to Ewing sarcoma combination chemotherapy

treatments.availability: Available at sarcoma/oncology centers — standard Ewing sarcoma protocol

treatments.name: Cisplatin + Doxorubicin + Cyclophosphamide (CAP Regimen)

evidence_level.rationale: Established empiric regimen for advanced salivary gland cancers. Multiple retrospective series. No specific data for myoepithelial carcinoma. ESMO guidelines note chemotherapy has limited activity in salivary gland cancers generally.

survival_benefit.rationale: Modest response rates in salivary gland cancers overall. Used as empiric palliative/adjuvant chemotherapy for high-risk disease. Salivary gland cancers generally have low chemosensitivity to platinum-based regimens. May be less effective than sarcoma-type regimens for EWSR1-rearranged tumors.

accessibility.rationale: All agents widely available. Standard chemotherapy combination. Well-established dosing protocols (cyclophosphamide 500 mg/m2, doxorubicin 50-80 mg/m2, cisplatin 50-80 mg/m2).

safety_profile.rationale: Triple-agent regimen with significant toxicity: cardiotoxicity (doxorubicin), nephrotoxicity and ototoxicity (cisplatin), myelosuppression (all agents), hemorrhagic cystitis (cyclophosphamide), severe nausea/vomiting.

biomarker_match.rationale: Non-targeted cytotoxic regimen. Not specific to EWSR1-rearranged biology. Traditional salivary gland cancer chemotherapy that may be less appropriate than sarcoma-type regimens for this specific molecular subtype.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Cisplatin forms platinum-DNA crosslinks causing DNA damage. Doxorubicin intercalates DNA and inhibits topoisomerase II. Cyclophosphamide alkylates DNA causing crosslinks. The CAP regimen provides synergistic cytotoxicity through three complementary mechanisms of DNA damage.

key_evidence.study_name: Chemotherapy for recurrent/metastatic salivary gland carcinoma — multiple retrospective series

treatments.name: Larotrectinib / Entrectinib (TRK Inhibitors) — If NTRK Fusion Present

evidence_level.rationale: FDA-approved for NTRK fusion-positive solid tumors (tissue-agnostic). ORR 92% in NTRK+ salivary gland cancers with larotrectinib (24 patients). However, this patient has EWSR1 rearrangement, NOT NTRK fusion. Would require additional molecular testing to identify potential NTRK fusion.

survival_benefit.rationale: Exceptional response rates in NTRK+ salivary gland cancers: 92% ORR with 13% CR. Durable responses. But only applicable if NTRK fusion is present, which is unlikely in EWSR1-rearranged myoepithelial carcinoma.

accessibility.rationale: FDA-approved for NTRK+ tumors. Oral formulation. Available at most oncology centers. Requires molecular confirmation of NTRK fusion.

safety_profile.rationale: Generally well-tolerated. Dizziness, fatigue, nausea, constipation, weight gain. Mild toxicity profile compared to cytotoxic chemotherapy. Suitable for long-term use.

biomarker_match.rationale: Requires NTRK gene fusion. This patient has EWSR1 rearrangement, which is a different fusion. NTRK fusions occur in ~5% of salivary gland cancers overall, primarily in secretory carcinoma (80% of cases). Unlikely to be present in EWSR1-rearranged myoepithelial carcinoma.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Larotrectinib and entrectinib selectively inhibit TRK proteins (TRKA, TRKB, TRKC) encoded by NTRK1, NTRK2, and NTRK3 genes. In tumors harboring NTRK gene fusions, the TRK protein is constitutively activated, driving proliferation and survival. TRK inhibitors block this oncogenic signaling. Entrectinib also inhibits ROS1 and ALK.

key_evidence.study_name: Larotrectinib Treatment for Patients with TRK Fusion-Positive Salivary Gland Cancers

biomarker_requirements: NTRK1/2/3 gene fusion (confirmed by FISH, IHC, or NGS)
Unlikely to be present in EWSR1-rearranged tumors

treatments.name: Pembrolizumab (Anti-PD-1 Immunotherapy) — For Recurrent/Metastatic Disease

evidence_level.rationale: KEYNOTE-028 Phase 1b in R/M salivary gland cancers showed modest activity (ORR 12%). No specific data for myoepithelial carcinoma. PD-L1 expression status unknown for this patient. May be considered if MSI-H/TMB-H is identified on molecular profiling.

survival_benefit.rationale: Modest response rates in salivary gland cancers overall. ORR 12% in KEYNOTE-028. Median PFS ~4 months. May be useful as salvage therapy for recurrent/metastatic disease after failure of surgery, RT, and chemotherapy.

accessibility.rationale: FDA-approved for multiple indications. Available at most oncology centers. Off-label for salivary gland cancers. Also approved for TMB-H (>=10 mutations/Mb) or MSI-H tumors regardless of histology.

safety_profile.rationale: Immune-related adverse events: thyroiditis, colitis, pneumonitis, hepatitis, dermatitis. Generally manageable. Better tolerated than cytotoxic chemotherapy.

biomarker_match.rationale: PD-L1 expression unknown. Myoepithelial carcinomas are not characterized as immune-inflamed tumors. TMB-H or MSI-H status should be assessed for eligibility. EWSR1 rearrangement does not predict immunotherapy response.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Pembrolizumab blocks the PD-1 receptor on T cells, restoring anti-tumor immune surveillance. By preventing PD-1/PD-L1 interaction, activated T cells recognize and destroy tumor cells. May be particularly effective in tumors with high TMB, MSI-H, or high PD-L1 expression.

key_evidence.study_name: KEYNOTE-028: Pembrolizumab for Advanced Salivary Gland Carcinoma

treatments.availability: FDA-approved for multiple indications; off-label for salivary gland cancer

treatments.name: Pazopanib (Multi-Kinase Inhibitor) — Salvage Therapy

evidence_level.rationale: Case report of complete remission of EWSR1-KLF15 myoepithelial carcinoma with pazopanib monotherapy. FDA-approved for soft tissue sarcoma. Phase 3 data in STS (PALETTE trial). No systematic data in salivary gland myoepithelial carcinoma.

survival_benefit.rationale: Case report of CR with pazopanib in EWSR1-rearranged myoepithelial carcinoma. In soft tissue sarcoma PALETTE trial, median PFS 4.6 months vs 1.6 months placebo. Potential for disease stabilization in EWSR1-rearranged tumors.

accessibility.rationale: FDA-approved for soft tissue sarcoma. Oral formulation. Available at oncology centers. Off-label for salivary gland cancer but EWSR1 rearrangement provides molecular rationale.

safety_profile.rationale: Hypertension, diarrhea, hepatotoxicity (requires LFT monitoring), fatigue, hand-foot syndrome, hair color changes, hypothyroidism. Significant but manageable toxicity profile.

biomarker_match.rationale: EWSR1-rearranged myoepithelial carcinoma has shown responsiveness to pazopanib in a case report. The shared biology with soft tissue sarcoma provides rationale. Multi-kinase inhibition targets VEGFR, PDGFR, and c-KIT (c-KIT is overexpressed in 80-90% of myoepithelial carcinomas).

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Pazopanib is a multi-kinase inhibitor targeting VEGFR1-3, PDGFR-alpha/beta, FGFR-1/3, and c-KIT. In myoepithelial carcinoma, c-KIT is overexpressed in 80-90% of cases. Anti-angiogenic and direct anti-proliferative effects reduce tumor growth. The EWSR1-rearranged molecular subtype may be particularly sensitive based on case-level evidence.

key_evidence.study_name: Complete remission of EWSR1-KLF15 myoepithelial carcinoma following pazopanib monotherapy (case report)

biomarker_requirements: c-KIT overexpression (present in 80-90% of myoepithelial carcinomas)
treatments.availability: FDA-approved for soft tissue sarcoma; off-label for salivary gland cancer

treatments.name: Comprehensive Molecular Profiling (NGS) for Basket Trial Enrollment

evidence_level.rationale: NGS molecular profiling identifies actionable mutations in 27% of salivary gland cancer patients. NCI-MATCH and other basket trials enroll based on molecular targets rather than histology. Particularly valuable for rare tumors with limited evidence base like myoepithelial carcinoma.

survival_benefit.rationale: Enables access to targeted therapies and clinical trials that would otherwise be unavailable. 14% of salivary gland cancer patients had potential biomarker-matched trial therapies. May identify actionable co-mutations beyond EWSR1.

accessibility.rationale: Available at most academic oncology centers. Foundation Medicine, Tempus, Guardant Health, and institutional NGS panels are widely available. Insurance coverage improving. Results in 2-3 weeks.

safety_profile.rationale: Diagnostic test with no treatment-related toxicity. Requires tumor tissue (archival or fresh biopsy) or liquid biopsy. No adverse effects.

biomarker_match.rationale: By definition, molecular profiling identifies the most relevant biomarkers for targeted therapy. May reveal co-occurring targetable alterations beyond EWSR1. Essential for rare tumors where empiric treatment options are limited.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Comprehensive genomic profiling using next-generation sequencing (NGS) analyzes hundreds of cancer-related genes simultaneously. Identifies actionable mutations, gene fusions, copy number alterations, microsatellite instability status, and tumor mutational burden. Results guide targeted therapy selection and clinical trial enrollment through basket trials that match molecular targets rather than tumor histology.

key_evidence.study_name: Evaluation of Clinical Utility of Genomic Profiling to Inform Selection of Clinical Trial Therapy in Salivary Gland Cancer

notable_side_effects: Potential for incidental findings requiring follow-up
treatments.availability: Available at most academic oncology centers and commercial labs

treatments.name: Cisplatin + Docetaxel — Empiric Chemotherapy

evidence_level.rationale: Phase 2 data in R/M salivary gland cancers showing ORR 46.5% and median PFS 7.2 months. Not specific to myoepithelial carcinoma. General salivary gland chemotherapy option.

survival_benefit.rationale: Moderate response rates with short PFS. Less theoretical rationale than sarcoma-type regimens for EWSR1-rearranged tumors. May be considered if patient declines or has contraindication to doxorubicin/ifosfamide.

accessibility.rationale: Both agents widely available. Standard chemotherapy combination.

safety_profile.rationale: Nephrotoxicity, ototoxicity, neuropathy (cisplatin), neutropenia (docetaxel), nausea/vomiting, alopecia. Significant toxicity burden.

biomarker_match.rationale: Non-targeted cytotoxic regimen. Not specific to EWSR1-rearranged biology. Sarcoma-type regimens may be more appropriate given the molecular profile.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Cisplatin forms platinum-DNA crosslinks causing DNA damage and apoptosis. Docetaxel stabilizes microtubules preventing cell division. Synergistic cytotoxicity through DNA damage and mitotic arrest.

key_evidence.study_name: Phase II Study of Cisplatin Plus Weekly Docetaxel in R/M Salivary Gland Cancer

treatments.name: Lenvatinib (Anti-VEGFR/FGFR TKI) — Salvage Therapy

evidence_level.rationale: Phase 2 data in R/M salivary gland cancers (primarily ACC). ORR 15.6%, disease control 90.6%, median PFS 17.5 months. Not specific to myoepithelial carcinoma.

survival_benefit.rationale: Primarily disease stabilization. May offer durable stable disease. Unknown activity specifically in myoepithelial carcinoma.

accessibility.rationale: FDA-approved for thyroid cancer. Oral formulation. Off-label for salivary gland cancers.

safety_profile.rationale: Hypertension, diarrhea, fatigue, proteinuria, palmar-plantar erythrodysesthesia, weight loss.

biomarker_match.rationale: Multi-kinase inhibitor. Not specific to EWSR1-rearranged biology. Anti-angiogenic mechanism may provide disease stabilization.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Multi-kinase inhibitor targeting VEGFR1-3, FGFR1-4, RET, KIT, and PDGFRalpha. Anti-angiogenic and anti-proliferative effects reduce tumor growth and vascularity.

key_evidence.study_name: Phase II Study of Lenvatinib in Progressive Salivary Gland Cancer

treatments.availability: FDA-approved for thyroid cancer; off-label for salivary gland cancer

treatments.name: Proton Beam Therapy (Adjuvant — Alternative to IMRT)

evidence_level.rationale: MSK Phase 2 trial showed proton therapy achieves similar outcomes with less toxicity for head and neck cancers including salivary gland tumors. Johns Hopkins case studies in salivary gland cancer. No specific data for myoepithelial carcinoma.

survival_benefit.rationale: Equivalent oncologic outcomes to IMRT. Reduced toxicity advantage (grade 2+ mucositis 7.5% vs 22.2%). For 45-year-old with long life expectancy, reduced late toxicity is meaningful.

accessibility.rationale: Limited to specialized proton centers. Higher cost. Insurance pre-authorization required.

safety_profile.rationale: Superior safety vs IMRT: reduced xerostomia, mucositis, second malignancy risk. Bragg peak dosimetry minimizes exit dose.

biomarker_match.rationale: Not biomarker-dependent. Applicable to all patients requiring adjuvant RT.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Proton beams deposit energy via Bragg peak, delivering maximum dose at tumor depth with minimal exit dose. Superior dose conformity compared to photon IMRT, reducing radiation exposure to surrounding normal tissues.

key_evidence.study_name: MSK Phase 2: Proton vs Photon for Head and Neck Cancer

clinical_trials.title: Phase II Study of Lenvatinib Plus Pembrolizumab in Recurrent/Metastatic Salivary Gland Cancers
biomarker_requirements: Recurrent or metastatic salivary gland cancer (all histologies)
clinical_trials.early_results: Combination of anti-VEGFR TKI + anti-PD-1. Results presented ASCO 2025.

clinical_trials.title: NCI-MATCH: Targeted Therapy Directed by Genetic Testing in Treating Patients with Advanced Refractory Solid Tumors
sites: United States — NCI-designated cancer centers nationwide
biomarker_requirements: Actionable molecular alteration identified by NGS
clinical_trials.early_results: Basket trial matching patients to therapies based on molecular alterations. Includes arms for various targetable mutations. Available for rare tumors like myoepithelial carcinoma.

clinical_trials.title: Larotrectinib in Patients with TRK Fusion-Positive Solid Tumors
clinical_trials.early_results: ORR 92% in NTRK+ salivary gland cancers. Only applicable if NTRK fusion is identified on molecular profiling.

clinical_trials.title: Pazopanib for Treatment of Advanced Soft Tissue Sarcoma (Including Myoepithelial Carcinoma with Sarcoma-Like Biology)
clinical_trials.status: FDA-approved for STS; off-label access for myoepithelial carcinoma
sites: Available at oncology centers treating soft tissue sarcomas
clinical_trials.early_results: Median PFS 4.6 months vs 1.6 months placebo in STS. Case report of CR in EWSR1-rearranged myoepithelial carcinoma.

clinical_trials.title: Testing Ado-Trastuzumab Emtansine in HER2-Positive Salivary Gland Cancer
sites: UCSF and other US academic centers
clinical_trials.early_results: Ongoing. For HER2+ salivary gland cancers only.

clinical_trials.trial_id: NCT (Proton vs IMRT in H&N — MSK)
clinical_trials.title: Proton Beam vs Photon IMRT in Head and Neck Cancers
biomarker_requirements: Head and neck cancer requiring RT
clinical_trials.early_results: Proton therapy: similar outcomes, less toxicity. Grade 2+ mucositis 7.5% vs 22.2% for IMRT.

combination_strategies.evidence_level: ASCO/ESMO Guidelines — Strong recommendation for high-risk features
combination_strategies.rationale: Standard multimodality approach for T3N1 high-grade salivary gland cancer. Surgery removes gross disease; adjuvant RT addresses microscopic residual disease. NCDB analysis shows improved survival with adjuvant RT in high-risk patients (HR 0.76). All high-risk features present: T3, N1, high-grade (Ki-67 25%).

combination_strategies.evidence_level: Case reports / Empiric — weak evidence but molecularly rational
combination_strategies.rationale: EWSR1 rearrangement creates sarcoma-like biology amenable to sarcoma chemotherapy. Case evidence of long-term survival with doxorubicin/ifosfamide + RT in myoepithelial carcinoma. Ki-67 25% suggests chemosensitivity. High risk of distant metastasis (22-33%) justifies systemic therapy. This combination addresses local (surgery+RT) and systemic (chemotherapy) disease.

For ultra-rare tumors like myoepithelial carcinoma with limited evidence base, molecular profiling identifies additional actionable targets beyond EWSR1. 27% of salivary gland cancer patients have genomically actionable alterations. NCI-MATCH and other basket trials provide access to targeted therapies matched to molecular profile.

Sequential or combination approach for EWSR1-rearranged myoepithelial carcinoma. Doxorubicin/ifosfamide for initial cytoreduction followed by pazopanib maintenance. Pazopanib targets c-KIT (overexpressed in 80-90% of myoepithelial carcinomas) and VEGFR. Case report of CR with pazopanib in EWSR1-rearranged myoepithelial carcinoma.

supportive_care.evidence: ASCO and head and neck surgery guidelines recommend facial nerve rehabilitation after radical parotidectomy. Options include nerve grafting (sural nerve graft), facial reanimation procedures, and physical therapy. Gold weight implant for eye closure if VII nerve paralysis occurs.
supportive_care.benefit: Restores facial symmetry, eye protection (prevents exposure keratopathy), and social functioning. Critical for quality of life in a 45-year-old. Nerve grafting provides potential for partial recovery over 12-18 months.

supportive_care.evidence: ESPEN guidelines for cancer patients receiving head and neck RT and/or chemotherapy. Proactive nutritional assessment prevents weight loss and treatment interruptions.
supportive_care.benefit: Maintains body weight and nutritional status during intensive multimodality treatment. Reduces risk of treatment interruptions. PEG tube placement should be considered if significant dysphagia anticipated.

supportive_care.evidence: ASCO and ESC guidelines for cardiovascular monitoring during anthracycline therapy. Serial echocardiography or MUGA scans at baseline and every 3 cycles.
supportive_care.benefit: Early detection of cardiotoxicity allows dose modification or cessation before irreversible cardiomyopathy develops. Critical for 45-year-old with long life expectancy. Dexrazoxane cardioprotection may be considered.

supportive_care.approach: Psychological Support and Cancer-Related Distress Management
supportive_care.evidence: NCCN Distress Management guidelines. Diagnosis of rare cancer with limited evidence base and facial surgery causes significant distress.
supportive_care.benefit: Addresses anxiety about rare diagnosis, facial appearance changes, and treatment uncertainty. Support groups for rare cancers. Coping strategies for body image changes after parotidectomy.

supportive_care.approach: Growth Factor Support (G-CSF) for Chemotherapy-Induced Neutropenia
supportive_care.evidence: ASCO/NCCN guidelines recommend primary G-CSF prophylaxis for regimens with >20% febrile neutropenia risk. Doxorubicin/ifosfamide and VDC/IE have high febrile neutropenia rates.
supportive_care.benefit: Reduces febrile neutropenia risk by 50-60%. Maintains dose intensity. Reduces hospitalization and infection risk. Essential for sarcoma-type chemotherapy regimens.
